title: Mamdani throws business leaders a curveball: Tapping antitrust crusader Lina Khan
description: Zohran Mamdani has tapped ally Lina Khan and McKinsey partner Anthony Shorris to lead New York City's Economic Development Corporation.

title: Students who attended 10 elite schools accused of price fixing are now getting payments from a $284 million settlement
description: Students who attended schools including Yale, Brown, and Columbia began receiving payments on July 20 from a financial aid antitrust settlement.
fullText:
Current and former students of 10 elite schools are receiving payments from a $284 million financial aid settlement.

Recipients are expected to receive an average of $2,000.

It follows an antitrust lawsuit that accused schools of participating in a "price-fixing cartel."

Check your bank account: you might be a couple of thousand dollars richer.

On July 20, the first batch of payments to current and former students who attended 10 elite schools was distributed from a $284 million settlement. It follows a 2022 lawsuit filed by students who accused the schools of participating in "a price-fixing cartel" that favored wealthy students and reduced financial aid packages for students, overcharging recipients "by at least hundreds of millions of dollars," according to the lawsuit.

The schools denied wrongdoing and agreed to make settlement payments to resolve the claims. According to the settlement website, current and former students who attended the following schools will receive payments of an average of $2,000: Brown, University of Chicago, Columbia, Dartmouth, Duke, Emory, Northwestern, Vanderbilt, Rice, and Yale.

Payments are being sent out through ACH, PayPal, Zelle, and Venmo. Those who opted for a physical check are expected to receive it by July 24.

Current and former students are expected to receive payments if they were enrolled in one of the listed schools' undergraduate programs full-time, received need-based financial aid from the school, and whose tuition and room and board costs were not fully covered by the financial aid they received.

Have a story to share? Contact this reporter at asheffey@businessinsider.com.

If you enjoyed this story, be sure to follow Business Insider on Yahoo.

title: SEC Commissioner Greg Sankey says talk of leaving the NCAA is "real"
description: As college football tries to navigate an era of chaos sparked by the reckoning from decades of antitrust violations, one of the most powerful conference claims it's serious about leaving the NCAA.
fullText:
As college football tries to navigate an era of chaos sparked by the reckoning from decades of antitrust violations, one of the most powerful conference claims it's serious about leaving the NCAA.

Via Nick Schultz of On3.com, SEC Commissioner Greg Sankey said Monday that his conference could indeed break away and do its own thing.

“You phrased the question, ‘How real?’ So I’ll take that as, like, real or not. So they’re real,” Sankey said. “People have talked about that. They’ve opined about the frustrations that bring them to the point of saying we should look at something significantly different."

Sankey claims he's not simply trying to secure an edge for the SEC in ongoing discussions regarding the future of college football.

“I do not believe that is a leverage point — I think that’s just honest communication," Sankey said. "I would prefer that stay in a meeting room or on a video conference and not be part of the D1 ticker the next morning, but I don’t control all of that.”

Leverage or not (and no leverage is worth a damn if the party exercising admits it's leverage), something has to give. And if the proposed solution harms the biggest conferences, why should they go along with it?

While recent years have seen a revolution in college football, the next phase could be an evolution in which major conferences run their own leagues, perhaps with (in time) a postseason tournament consisting of the best teams in each conference.

With the NCAA losing much of its power, the conferences can fill the void. With so many college football teams, maybe the best solution is to have several different leagues that run their own businesses. The various conferences can then do their own thing. Once the conferences are sorted out, the postseason arrangements will follow.
